title: A deploy script nuked the file permissions
problem: A junior engineer ran 'chmod -R 777 /var/www/html' to fix a deploy issue. Now every file is world-writable, nginx is refusing to serve pages, and your security team is paging you. Restore correct permissions across the whole directory tree without touching each file by hand.

setup.description: Create a sample web root and apply the broken 777 permissions to simulate the incident.
setup.note: On RHEL/CentOS the group may be 'nobody' instead of 'nogroup' and the web user is 'nginx' or 'apache' instead of 'www-data'.

hints: Directories and files need different permissions — directories need the execute bit (x) so you can cd into them, but regular files should not be executable unless they are scripts.
Use find with -type d and -type f to target only directories or only files, then run chmod on each match using -exec. This avoids setting the same mode on everything.
The safe defaults for a public web directory are 755 for directories and 644 for files. Run: find /var/www/html -type d -exec chmod 755 {} \; and then the equivalent for files with 644.
solution:
1. Inspect the damage:
   ls -laR /var/www/html
   # Every entry shows rwxrwxrwx (777) — world-writable.

2. Fix directory permissions:
   sudo find /var/www/html -type d -exec chmod 755 {} \;
   # 755 = owner can write, group and others can read and traverse.
   # Directories need the execute bit so users can cd into them.

3. Fix file permissions:
   sudo find /var/www/html -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \;
   # 644 = owner can write, everyone else can only read.
   # Files should not be executable unless they are shell scripts.

4. Restore ownership to the web server user:
   sudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/html
   # On RHEL/CentOS use: sudo chown -R nginx:nginx /var/www/html

5. Verify the result:
   ls -laR /var/www/html
   # Directories should show drwxr-xr-x, files -rw-r--r--.

6. Why 777 is dangerous:
   Any user on the server — or a compromised process — can overwrite
   your HTML, inject scripts, or replace binaries. Web servers like
   nginx also refuse to serve files owned by others when they are
   world-writable, as a security policy.
